concepts: Node.js executes JavaScript using the V8 engine
Node.js provides runtime APIs for filesystem networking processes and other server-side operations
JavaScript execution is primarily single-threaded
Node.js can handle many concurrent operations through asynchronous I/O
libuv provides event loop and asynchronous I/O infrastructure
questionPatterns: How does Node.js work internally?
If JavaScript runs on one main thread, how can Node.js handle thousands of concurrent requests?
evaluationCriteria: mentions V8 for JavaScript execution
explains asynchronous non-blocking I/O
understands that single-threaded JavaScript execution does not mean the entire Node.js runtime uses only one thread
mentions the role of libuv

concepts: event loop coordinates asynchronous callback execution
event loop progresses through multiple phases
timers phase handles eligible timer callbacks
poll phase processes many I/O callbacks
check phase handles setImmediate callbacks
microtasks are processed at specific points between callback execution
questionPatterns: Explain the Node.js event loop.
What are the major phases of the Node.js event loop?
evaluationCriteria: understands the event loop coordinates asynchronous work
names important phases such as timers poll and check
does not claim asynchronous operations all execute directly on the JavaScript thread

concepts: libuv provides cross-platform asynchronous I/O support
some operations use operating system asynchronous facilities
some operations are delegated to the libuv thread pool
filesystem operations and some crypto and DNS operations can use the thread pool
thread pool saturation can affect latency
questionPatterns: What is libuv and why does Node.js use it?
Does every asynchronous operation in Node.js use the thread pool?
evaluationCriteria: understands libuv supports the Node.js asynchronous runtime
does not claim every asynchronous operation uses the thread pool
can name common thread-pool workloads
understands thread-pool saturation can become a bottleneck
followUpConcepts: CPU Bound vs IO Bound Work

topic: CPU Bound vs IO Bound Work
concepts: I/O-bound work spends significant time waiting for external operations
CPU-bound work spends significant time performing computation
Node.js asynchronous architecture is effective for many I/O-heavy workloads
CPU-intensive JavaScript can block the event loop
worker threads or external services can handle heavy computation
questionPatterns: Why is Node.js considered good for I/O-heavy applications?
What happens if you perform a large CPU-intensive calculation inside an API request handler?
evaluationCriteria: correctly distinguishes CPU-bound and I/O-bound work
understands synchronous CPU-heavy work blocks JavaScript execution
suggests appropriate offloading approaches

concepts: long-running synchronous JavaScript prevents other callbacks from executing
large synchronous loops can block request processing
synchronous filesystem operations can block the main thread
event loop lag increases application latency
CPU-heavy work should be broken up or moved elsewhere when appropriate
questionPatterns: What does it mean to block the Node.js event loop?
Why should synchronous filesystem APIs generally be avoided inside request handlers?
evaluationCriteria: understands blocking prevents other JavaScript callbacks from progressing
identifies CPU-heavy and synchronous operations as common causes
connects blocking to increased latency across concurrent requests
followUpConcepts: CPU Bound vs IO Bound Work

concepts: process.nextTick uses a Node.js-specific next-tick queue
Promise callbacks use the microtask queue
nextTick callbacks are processed before regular Promise microtasks in common Node.js execution checkpoints
excessive recursive nextTick usage can delay event loop progress
questionPatterns: What's the difference between process.nextTick and Promise.resolve().then()?
Why can recursively scheduling process.nextTick cause problems?
evaluationCriteria: distinguishes the next-tick queue from Promise microtasks
understands nextTick callbacks receive very high scheduling priority
connects excessive nextTick scheduling to event-loop starvation

concepts: setTimeout schedules a callback after a minimum delay threshold
setImmediate schedules execution in the check phase
execution order can depend on the context in which they are scheduled
neither should be treated as universally executing first
questionPatterns: What's the difference between setImmediate and setTimeout with zero delay?
Which executes first: setImmediate or setTimeout?
evaluationCriteria: does not claim one always executes first
connects setImmediate to the check phase
understands timers execute after their threshold becomes eligible

topic: CommonJS vs ES Modules in Node.js
concepts: CommonJS uses require and module.exports
ES modules use import and export
package configuration and file extensions can determine module interpretation
ES modules support static module structure
the two systems have interoperability differences
questionPatterns: What's the difference between CommonJS and ES modules in Node.js?
Why might require not work inside an ES module?
evaluationCriteria: correctly identifies both module systems
understands module mode affects available syntax and globals
recognizes interoperability requires deliberate handling

concepts: loaded CommonJS modules are generally cached after first evaluation
subsequent require calls can reuse cached exports
module-level state can therefore behave like shared process state
cache behavior depends on resolved module identity
questionPatterns: Does Node.js execute a required module every time you call require?
Why can module-level variables retain state across multiple imports?
evaluationCriteria: understands modules are generally evaluated once per resolved cached module
connects caching to shared module state
recognizes caching is process-local
followUpConcepts: CommonJS vs ES Modules in Node.js

concepts: package.json stores project metadata scripts and dependency declarations
dependencies are required by the application at runtime
devDependencies are generally development-time dependencies
package-lock.json records resolved dependency versions
semantic version ranges affect dependency updates
questionPatterns: What's the difference between dependencies and devDependencies?
Why should package-lock.json usually be committed?
evaluationCriteria: correctly distinguishes runtime and development dependencies
understands lockfiles improve reproducibility
has basic awareness of semantic versioning

concepts: streams process data incrementally
readable writable duplex and transform streams
streams avoid loading entire large payloads into memory
pipe connects compatible stream flows
backpressure prevents producers from overwhelming consumers
questionPatterns: Why would you use streams to process a large file?
What are the main types of streams in Node.js?
evaluationCriteria: understands incremental data processing
names major stream types
connects streams to memory efficiency
recognizes backpressure as an important stream concern

concepts: producer can generate data faster than consumer processes it
uncontrolled buffering increases memory usage
writable stream write can signal when producers should pause
drain indicates writing can continue
pipe and pipeline help manage flow control
questionPatterns: What is backpressure in Node.js streams?
What happens if a readable stream produces data much faster than a writable stream can process it?
evaluationCriteria: correctly identifies producer-consumer speed mismatch
connects uncontrolled buffering to memory growth
understands flow control is required

concepts: Buffer represents binary data
commonly used with files network packets and streams
buffers contain raw bytes
encoding converts between binary data and textual representations
questionPatterns: What is a Buffer in Node.js?
Why do file uploads often involve buffers?
evaluationCriteria: understands buffers represent binary data
connects buffers to I/O operations
distinguishes raw bytes from JavaScript strings

concepts: fs module provides filesystem operations
callback Promise and synchronous APIs are available
synchronous operations block JavaScript execution
large files should often be streamed rather than fully loaded into memory
questionPatterns: What's the difference between readFile and createReadStream?
Why should you avoid readFileSync inside a request handler?
evaluationCriteria: understands synchronous filesystem operations block execution
selects streaming for large files
can use asynchronous APIs for request-time operations

concepts: EventEmitter implements an event-driven publish-subscribe style API within a process
listeners subscribe using on or once
events are emitted using emit
listeners run synchronously when an event is emitted unless they schedule asynchronous work
questionPatterns: What is EventEmitter in Node.js?
Are EventEmitter listeners automatically asynchronous?
evaluationCriteria: understands event registration and emission
knows listeners are invoked synchronously by emit
provides a reasonable event-driven use case

concepts: worker threads execute JavaScript in separate threads
useful for CPU-intensive JavaScript workloads
workers have separate JavaScript execution contexts
communication can use message passing
shared memory is possible using SharedArrayBuffer
questionPatterns: When would you use worker_threads in Node.js?
Would worker threads make normal database API calls faster?
evaluationCriteria: connects workers primarily to CPU-heavy JavaScript work
does not recommend workers automatically for normal asynchronous I/O
understands communication introduces overhead
followUpConcepts: CPU Bound vs IO Bound Work

concepts: multiple Node.js processes can utilize multiple CPU cores
each process has separate memory
traffic can be distributed between workers
in-memory state is not automatically shared
external shared stores are needed for shared distributed state
questionPatterns: How can a Node.js application use multiple CPU cores?
What happens to in-memory session data when your application runs across multiple processes?
evaluationCriteria: understands multiple processes have separate memory
connects process scaling to external shared state
distinguishes clustering from worker threads

concepts: middleware functions participate in the request-response pipeline
middleware receives request response and next
middleware order matters
middleware can modify request or response objects
common uses include authentication validation logging and error handling
questionPatterns: What is middleware in Express?
Why does middleware order matter?
evaluationCriteria: correctly explains the middleware pipeline
understands next passes control forward
recognizes incorrect ordering can cause security or functionality problems

concepts: incoming request enters middleware stack
middleware executes according to registration and route matching
route handler processes matched requests
response ends the request lifecycle
errors can be forwarded to error-handling middleware
questionPatterns: Walk me through what happens when a request reaches an Express application.
What happens if middleware neither sends a response nor calls next?
evaluationCriteria: explains middleware and route execution order
understands a request can remain pending if control is not continued or completed
recognizes error middleware as part of centralized failure handling

concepts: centralized error-handling middleware
error middleware uses four parameters
operational errors should be converted into appropriate responses
internal implementation details should not be exposed in production
asynchronous errors must reach the error-handling pipeline
questionPatterns: How would you implement centralized error handling in Express?
Why is centralized error middleware better than repeating try-catch response logic everywhere?
evaluationCriteria: understands the error middleware signature
centralizes consistent error responses
distinguishes expected operational errors from unexpected failures
avoids leaking sensitive stack traces

concepts: rejected asynchronous operations must reach error middleware
behavior differs between Express versions and handler styles
wrapper utilities can forward rejected Promises in older patterns
unhandled asynchronous failures should not silently disappear
questionPatterns: How do you handle errors from async Express route handlers?
Why can asynchronous error handling differ from synchronous error handling?
evaluationCriteria: understands rejected Promises must be handled or forwarded
recognizes framework-version differences
uses a consistent centralized strategy

concepts: routes map HTTP methods and paths to handlers
Express Router enables modular route organization
route parameters represent path values
query parameters commonly represent filtering sorting or pagination

evaluationCriteria: correctly distinguishes path and query parameters

concepts: server authenticates credentials
server signs a token containing claims
client presents the token on later requests
server verifies signature and relevant claims
JWT payload is encoded rather than inherently encrypted
questionPatterns: Walk me through JWT authentication in a Node.js application.

evaluationCriteria: correctly explains signing and verification
understands JWT payloads can be decoded

concepts: access tokens are generally short-lived
refresh tokens obtain new access tokens
refresh token storage requires stronger protection
token rotation can reduce replay risk
logout and revocation strategies must be deliberately designed
questionPatterns: Why use refresh tokens instead of making access tokens valid for months?

evaluationCriteria: understands short-lived access tokens reduce exposure
explains refresh flow correctly

concepts: HttpOnly prevents JavaScript from directly reading a cookie
Secure restricts transmission to HTTPS
SameSite controls some cross-site cookie behavior
cookie-based authentication requires consideration of CSRF
XSS and CSRF are different threats
questionPatterns: Why would you store an authentication token in an HttpOnly cookie?

evaluationCriteria: understands HttpOnly reduces direct token theft through JavaScript

does not claim HttpOnly prevents XSS itself

concepts: passwords should be hashed rather than encrypted for normal authentication storage

cost factor controls computational expense
password comparison hashes the candidate according to the stored hash parameters
questionPatterns: Why do you use bcrypt instead of storing encrypted passwords?

evaluationCriteria: understands password hashing is intentionally one-way

concepts: CORS is a browser-enforced cross-origin access mechanism
server returns headers describing allowed origins methods and headers
some requests trigger a preflight OPTIONS request
credentialed requests require compatible origin and credential configuration
questionPatterns: What is CORS and why does a Node.js backend need to configure it?

concepts: limits request frequency
protects APIs from abuse and accidental overload
common algorithms include fixed window sliding window and token bucket
distributed deployments require shared rate-limit state
client identity must be determined carefully behind proxies
questionPatterns: Why would you add rate limiting to a Node.js API?

recognizes proxy configuration can affect client IP detection

concepts: production applications commonly run behind reverse proxies or platform load balancers
proxy headers can communicate original client information

incorrect trust configuration can create security or rate-limiting problems
questionPatterns: Why does Express have a trust proxy setting?

concepts: Helmet configures multiple HTTP security headers

security headers provide defense in depth
security configuration should match application requirements

concepts: never trust client-provided input
schema validation checks expected shape and constraints
validation should happen at application boundaries

concepts: multipart form data

concepts: backend receives or streams uploaded data

concepts: scheduled jobs execute tasks according to a configured schedule
useful for periodic maintenance and notifications

concepts: long-running non-immediate tasks can be moved outside request handling

concepts: configuration should be separated from source code

concepts: unbounded global collections
forgotten timers

closures retaining large objects

concepts: uncaught exceptions indicate failures that escaped normal handling

concepts: supports bidirectional event-based communication
